section.title: Hide Me from the Wicked
section.sectionRationale: A lament against secret slander, where enemies sharpen their tongues like swords and shoot bitter words like arrows from ambush, this psalm prays for protection from hidden schemes and turns, once God acts, into confident hope that the very weapon the wicked aimed at others will be turned back upon them.
section.thesis: Against enemies who plot in secret, confident no one sees their hidden words, David prays to be hidden by God instead, trusting that the same God who sees every secret scheme will suddenly turn the wicked's own weapons against them, so that the righteous rejoice not in vengeance but in God's public vindication.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the plea for protection from the hidden schemes of the wicked who sharpen their tongues like swords, verses 1-6; and confidence that God will suddenly turn their own arrows back upon them, so that all mankind fears and the righteous rejoice, verses 7-10.

units.label: Hide me from the scheming of the wicked

verses.text: Hear, O God, my voice of complaint; preserve my life from dread of the enemy.
Hide me from the scheming of the wicked, from the mob of workers of iniquity,
who sharpen their {{lashon|tongues}} like swords and aim their bitter words like arrows,
ambushing the innocent in seclusion, shooting suddenly, without fear.
They hold fast to their evil purpose; they speak of hiding their snares. "Who will see them?" they say.
They devise injustice and say, "We have perfected a secret plan." For the inner man and the heart are {{amoq|mysterious}}.

units.label: God will shoot them with arrows
units.note: confidence that their own weapon will turn against them

verses.text: But God will shoot them with arrows; suddenly they will be wounded.
They will be made to stumble, their own tongues turned against them. All who see will shake their heads.
Then all mankind will fear and proclaim the work of God; so they will ponder what He has done.
Let the righteous rejoice in the Lord and take refuge in Him; let all the upright in heart exult.

lashon.gloss: Tongue, speech, language. Here sharpened like a sword, it pictures words used deliberately as a weapon, planned ambush rather than careless offense, exposing how deadly speech itself can be even without physical violence.

amoq.gloss: Deep, mysterious, hidden from ordinary sight. The wicked believe their inner plans are too deep for anyone to trace, verse 6, a confidence the psalm exposes as false before the God who sees every hidden thing.

basic.context: David faces enemies who do not attack openly but plot secretly, sharpening their words like swords and hiding their traps, confident that no one, not even God, sees their hidden schemes. He prays for protection, and the psalm turns to confidence that God will suddenly expose and judge them with their own weapon.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm addresses the particular danger of hidden slander and secret plotting, not open physical attack, and shows that no scheme is actually hidden from God, however confident the wicked feel in their secrecy.
basic.originalLanguages: The wicked sharpen their lashon, tongue, like a sword, turning speech itself into ambush. They believe their heart's plans are amoq, too deep or mysterious to be traced, a confidence the psalm exposes as false before God.
basic.typology: The pattern of the wicked plotting in secret, confident no one sees, while God sees and will expose them, anticipates the final judgment when 'nothing is covered that will not be revealed, or hidden that will not be known,' Luke 12:2.
basic.christ: Christ Himself endured secret plotting from religious leaders who conspired in hidden councils to destroy Him, Matthew 26:3-4, yet He taught that every hidden thing will be revealed, Luke 12:2-3, the very confidence this psalm expresses that no scheme, however carefully concealed, escapes God's sight.
basic.god: God is shown as the one who sees every hidden scheme however deep or secret, who can suddenly turn the wicked's own weapon back upon them, and whose public acts of judgment lead all mankind, not just the righteous, to fear and ponder His works.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 12:2-3 teaches that nothing hidden will remain concealed, echoing this psalm's confidence that God sees secret schemes. Matthew 26:3-4 records religious leaders secretly plotting against Jesus. Galatians 6:7 affirms that whatever a person sows, secretly or openly, they will also reap.
basic.oneStory: David's confidence that God sees and will expose every hidden scheme against the innocent finds its climax in Christ, who endured secret plotting from His enemies yet promised that every hidden thing will be revealed, and whose own resurrection publicly vindicated the righteous one His enemies thought they had silenced.

context.literary: A two-part lament moving from complaint about the wicked's hidden, weapon-like speech and secret schemes, verses 1-6, to confident assurance that God will suddenly expose and judge them, leading all mankind to fear and the righteous to rejoice, verses 7-10.
context.historical: Its setting is not tied to one narrated event but reflects a recurring danger David faced from courtiers, rivals, or slanderers plotting against him in secret rather than open battle, a different kind of threat than the psalms describing armed pursuit.
context.canonical: It belongs to a group of psalms exposing the deceit of the tongue as a serious moral and spiritual danger, alongside Psalm 12's 'flattering lips' and Psalm 52's 'deceitful tongue,' treating slander as a real weapon requiring real divine defense.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the specific danger of hidden slander and secret plotting before God, and to declare confidently that no scheme, however deep or carefully concealed, escapes His sight or His justice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a particular experience of secret verbal attack and ambush; it prescribes as an abiding pattern bringing hidden injustice honestly to God rather than despairing that wickedness done in secret will never be exposed.

debates.question: Does verse 8, 'their own tongues turned against them,' describe God's direct, active judgment, or the natural, self-destructive consequence of the wicked's own scheming?

views.label: The verse describes God's direct, sovereign intervention, He actively shoots them with arrows, verse 7, and turns their weapon against them as a deliberate act of judgment.
views.case: Verse 7 explicitly names God as the active subject, 'God will shoot them with arrows,' making the reversal in verse 8 a continuation of His direct judgment rather than an impersonal process.

views.label: The verse describes the natural, self-destructive outcome built into deceit itself, schemes based on lies tend to collapse and expose their own makers even without a distinct additional act of judgment.
views.case: Wisdom literature elsewhere describes this pattern as built into the moral order itself, 'whoever digs a pit will fall into it,' Proverbs 26:27, suggesting the reversal can be read as the natural unraveling of deceit.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the wicked's own words and schemes become the very means of their downfall; whether by direct divine act or through the moral order God has built into creation, deceit does not, in the end, escape exposure.

study.originalLanguages: The wicked sharpen their lashon, tongue, like a sword, turning speech itself into a weapon of planned ambush. They believe the schemes of the heart are amoq, too deep or hidden to be traced, a false confidence the psalm exposes before the God who sees every secret thing.

typology.type: The wicked plotting in secret, confident that no one, not even God, sees their schemes, verses 5-6
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the final judgment when 'nothing is covered that will not be revealed, or hidden that will not be known,' Luke 12:2.

typology.type: The wicked's own weapon turned back upon them, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the pattern of God's justice throughout Scripture, culminating in the cross, where the very plot to destroy the righteous one became the means of His triumph, Colossians 2:14-15.

study.christ: Christ Himself endured secret plotting from religious leaders who conspired in hidden councils to destroy Him, Matthew 26:3-4, yet He taught that every hidden thing will be revealed, Luke 12:2-3, the very confidence this psalm expresses that no scheme, however carefully concealed, escapes God's sight. And the pattern of verse 8, the wicked's own weapon turned back upon them, finds its deepest fulfillment at the cross, where the very plot to destroy the righteous one became, in God's hands, the means of triumph over every hidden power of evil, Colossians 2:14-15.
study.god: God is shown as the one who sees every hidden scheme however deep or secret, who can suddenly turn the wicked's own weapon back upon them, and whose public acts of judgment lead all mankind, not just the righteous, to fear and ponder His works.

crossReferences.note: Teaches that nothing hidden will remain concealed, echoing this psalm's confidence that God sees secret schemes.
Records religious leaders secretly plotting against Jesus, a pattern this psalm describes and God ultimately overturns.
Shows the plot to destroy Christ becoming, in God's hands, the means of triumph over every hidden scheme of evil.

study.oneStory: David's confidence that God sees and will expose every hidden scheme against the innocent finds its climax in Christ, who endured secret plotting from His enemies yet promised that every hidden thing will be revealed, and whose own triumph over the cross's hidden plot publicly vindicated the righteous one His enemies thought they had silenced.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the wicked's speech, in verse 3?

options.text: They sharpen their tongues like swords and aim bitter words like arrows.
options.note: This image treats deliberate, hidden slander as a real, deadly weapon.

options.text: They speak carelessly without any real intent to harm.
options.note: The psalm describes deliberate, planned attack, not careless speech.

options.text: They remain completely silent toward their enemies.
options.note: The psalm's enemies actively speak, using words as weapons, not silence.

options.text: They openly declare their accusations in public court.
options.note: The psalm emphasizes secrecy and ambush, not open, public accusation.

quiz.q: What false confidence do the wicked hold, according to verses 5-6?

options.text: That their secret schemes are too deep for anyone, even God, to see or trace.
options.note: This confidence is exposed as false by the psalm's turn to God's certain justice.

options.text: That God will reward their honesty.
options.note: The wicked are not being honest; they are hiding and scheming in secret.

options.text: That their plans will fail immediately.
options.note: The wicked believe the opposite, that their perfected secret plan will succeed.

options.text: That other people will eventually help them succeed.
options.note: Their confidence is specifically in the secrecy and undetectability of their own scheme.

quiz.q: What happens to the wicked's own weapon, according to verse 8?

options.text: Their own tongues are turned against them.
options.note: The very weapon they used, their scheming speech, becomes the instrument of their downfall.

options.text: It is simply taken away with no further consequence.
options.note: The verse specifically describes their own tongues turning against them, not a neutral removal.

options.text: It is given to the righteous to use instead.
options.note: The psalm does not describe the righteous adopting the same weapon; it describes the wicked's downfall by it.

options.text: It is forgotten and never mentioned again.
options.note: The reversal is a central, deliberate outcome of the psalm, not something forgotten.

quiz.q: What effect does God's action have on 'all mankind,' according to verse 9?

options.text: They fear and ponder the work God has done.
options.note: God's public vindication of the righteous leads even those beyond His people to recognize and reflect on His work.

options.text: They immediately rebel further against God.
options.note: The verse describes fear and reflection, not further rebellion.

options.text: They remain completely unaware anything happened.
options.note: The verse specifically says mankind will see, fear, and proclaim what God has done.

options.text: Only the psalmist personally notices what happened.
options.note: The verse broadens the effect to 'all mankind,' not just the psalmist alone.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's confidence that hidden schemes will be exposed relate to the teaching of Jesus?

options.text: Jesus taught that nothing hidden will remain concealed, that all will be revealed.
options.note: Luke 12:2-3 directly parallels this psalm's confidence that no secret scheme escapes final exposure.

options.text: Jesus taught that secret sins are permanently hidden from God.
options.note: Jesus explicitly taught the opposite, that every hidden thing will be revealed.

options.text: Jesus taught that only public sins matter, not private scheming.
options.note: Jesus' teaching in Luke 12 addresses precisely what is spoken in private or secret.

options.text: Jesus never addressed the topic of hidden or secret wrongdoing.
options.note: Luke 12:2-3 directly addresses hidden things being revealed, a clear teaching on this topic.

questions: David faced enemies who plotted against him in secret, confident no one would ever discover their scheme. Have you experienced hidden slander or scheming against you, and how does bringing it honestly to God, rather than trying to expose it yourself, change your response?
The wicked believed their inner plans were too deep or mysterious for anyone to trace, verse 6. Where might you be tempted to believe that your own secret thoughts or plans are hidden from God?

The psalm shows the wicked's own weapon, their scheming tongue, eventually turned against them, verse 8. Where do you need to trust God's timing for exposing injustice rather than taking justice into your own hands right now?

God's public action leads 'all mankind' to fear and ponder His work, not just the psalmist, verse 9. How does remembering that God's justice is ultimately public, not merely private, shape how you wait for Him to act?

The psalm closes with an invitation for the righteous to rejoice and take refuge in the Lord, verse 10. What would it look like for you to actively rejoice in God today, even while an injustice against you remains unresolved?

translationNotes.note: Tongues renders lashon, ordinary speech, here sharpened like a sword, a deliberate image treating slander and hidden accusation as a genuine weapon.
Mysterious renders amoq, deep or hidden, describing the wicked's false confidence that their inner schemes are beyond anyone's, even God's, ability to trace.
Made to stumble, their own tongues turned against them, pictures the wicked's own words becoming evidence or cause of their downfall, a reversal of their intended ambush.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via bible.com (YouVersion, Berean Standard Bible, version 3034) after the local makor-scripture toolkit failed with a persistent disk I/O error reading the sandbox-mounted database. Verse numbering and count (10 verses) verified against Psalm 64:1-64:10 per PSALMS-SECTION-MAP.md. The musical superscription is not rendered as verse text, consistent with the established convention in this corpus. No em or en dashes present. No sermon link could be verified this session from the trusted cluster for Psalm 64 specifically, so sermon status is none.
